subject: ap-chemistry
title: Gas Laws & Properties
stimulus: A student is doing experiments with CO

parts.prompt:
Describe the effect of raising the temperature on the motion of the CO
(g) molecules.
parts.scoring_notes: At higher temperature, CO$_2$ molecules have greater average kinetic energy → they move faster (higher average speed). Temperature is directly proportional to the average kinetic energy of gas particles: $\bar{KE} = \frac{3}{2}kT$. Higher T → higher average speed of CO$_2$ molecules.

scoring_points.description: For stating that CO$_2$ molecules move faster (higher average kinetic energy/speed) at higher temperature: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: The average speed of the molecules increases as temperature increases. 1 point is earned for the correct answer.

sample_responses.response: Raising the temperature increases the average kinetic energy of CO$_2$ molecules, causing them to move at higher average speeds.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point: correctly states that average speed (and kinetic energy) increases with temperature.

sample_responses.response: The molecules vibrate faster in place.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point: for ideal gas behavior the relevant effect is translational speed (motion through space), not molecular vibration.

required_elements: The average speed (or average kinetic energy) of CO$_2$ molecules increases as temperature increases.
alternatives.correct_example: Raising the temperature increases the average kinetic energy of CO$_2$ molecules, causing them to move at higher average speeds. The distribution of speeds broadens and shifts to higher values.

wrong_examples: Saying all molecules move at the same speed — kinetic molecular theory describes a distribution of speeds, not a single speed.
Saying molecules vibrate more — for ideal gas behavior, the primary effect is translational kinetic energy (speed), not vibration.
common_traps: Temperature ∝ average kinetic energy. KE = ½mv² → higher T → higher average v (speed). Always state 'average' speed since there is a distribution.

parts.prompt:
Calculate the pressure of the CO
(g) in the container at 425 K.
parts.scoring_notes: Since the volume is rigid (constant V) and the number of moles is constant, use Gay-Lussac's law: P$_1$/T$_1$ = P$_2$/T$_2$. Initial conditions: P$_1$ = 0.70 atm, T$_1$ = 299 K. Final T$_2$ = 425 K. P$_2$ = P$_1$ × T$_2$/T$_1$ = (0.70)(425/299) = 0.70 × 1.421 = 0.995 ≈ 0.99 atm.

scoring_points.description: For the correct pressure ≈ 0.99 atm using Gay-Lussac's law (P$_1$/T$_1$ = P$_2$/T$_2$): 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: Both the volume and the number of molecules are constant, therefore $\frac{P_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2}{T_2}$; $\frac{0.70\text{ atm}}{299\text{ K}} = \frac{P_2}{425\text{ K}}$; $P_2 = 0.99\text{ atm}$. 1 point is earned for the correct answer.

sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point: correct Gay-Lussac's law calculation with Kelvin temperatures.
Does not earn the point: used temperature in Celsius (25°C for T$_1$ and 425°C for T$_2$) instead of Kelvin (299 K and 425 K). Must convert to Kelvin.

required_elements: $P_2 = P_1 \times T_2/T_1 = (0.70\text{ atm})(425\text{ K}/299\text{ K}) \approx 0.99\text{ atm}$
alternatives.correct_example:
Constant V and n: $P_1/T_1 = P_2/T_2$
$P_2 = (0.70\text{ atm}) \times (425\text{ K}/299\text{ K}) = 0.70 \times 1.42 \approx 0.99\text{ atm}$

wrong_examples: Using T in Celsius — must convert to Kelvin. T$_1$ = 26°C = 299 K (not 26); T$_2$ = 425 K.
Using P ∝ V (Boyle's law) — for constant V, use Gay-Lussac's law (P/T = constant), not Boyle's law.
common_traps: Gay-Lussac's law: at constant V and n, P/T = constant → P$_2$ = P$_1$(T$_2$/T$_1$). Temperature MUST be in Kelvin.

parts.prompt:
In terms of kinetic molecular theory, briefly explain why the pressure of the CO
(g) in the container
changes as it is heated to 425 K.
parts.scoring_notes: At higher temperature, CO$_2$ molecules move faster (greater average kinetic energy). Faster-moving molecules collide with the container walls more frequently AND more forcefully. More frequent and/or more forceful collisions result in greater pressure.

scoring_points.description: For stating that faster molecules collide more frequently and/or forcefully with the walls → higher pressure: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: Faster-moving gas particles collide more frequently with the walls of the container, thus increasing the pressure. OR Faster-moving gas particles collide more forcefully with the walls of the container, thus increasing the pressure. 1 point is earned for a correct explanation.

sample_responses.response: At 425 K, CO$_2$ molecules have greater average kinetic energy and higher average speed. Faster-moving molecules collide with the container walls more frequently and with greater force per collision. Since pressure = force/area, more frequent and forceful collisions produce higher pressure.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point: correctly links higher temperature to faster molecular speeds and more/harder wall collisions causing higher pressure.

sample_responses.response: The pressure increases because there are more molecules at higher temperature.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point: the number of molecules is constant (rigid sealed container). The pressure increases because the existing molecules move faster and hit the walls more forcefully/frequently.

required_elements: At higher temperature, molecules have greater average speed.
Molecules collide with the container walls more frequently (and/or more forcefully) → greater pressure.
alternatives.correct_example: At 425 K, CO$_2$ molecules have greater average kinetic energy and move faster. Faster-moving molecules strike the container walls more frequently and with greater force. Pressure is due to the force per unit area of molecular collisions — more frequent and forceful collisions result in higher pressure.

wrong_examples: Saying more molecules are present — the container is sealed and no molecules are added; n is constant.
Saying pressure increases because volume decreases — the container is rigid (constant V).
common_traps: KMT explanation of pressure change with T: higher T → faster molecules → more and harder wall collisions → higher pressure. Volume and moles are unchanged.

parts.prompt:
The student measures the actual pressure of the CO
(g) in the container at 425 K and observes that it is less
than the pressure predicted by the ideal gas law. Explain this observation.
parts.scoring_notes: Real gases deviate from ideal behavior due to intermolecular attractions and finite molecular volume. CO$_2$ is a polar molecule with significant London dispersion forces and dipole-induced dipole interactions. The attractive forces between CO$_2$ molecules slow them down slightly before they hit the walls, reducing the force of collisions. This means real pressure is less than predicted by ideal gas law.

scoring_points.description: For identifying intermolecular attractive forces between CO$_2$ molecules reducing pressure below ideal: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: The attractive forces between CO$_2$ molecules result in a pressure that is lower than that predicted by the ideal gas law. 1 point is earned for a correct explanation.

sample_responses.response: CO$_2$ molecules experience intermolecular attractive forces (London dispersion and dipole-induced dipole). These attractions pull molecules away from the container walls slightly before impact, reducing the force of each collision. As a result, actual pressure is lower than the ideal gas law predicts (which assumes no IMF).
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point: correctly identifies IMF attractions as the cause of reduced pressure below ideal.

sample_responses.response: CO$_2$ molecules are large and take up space, reducing the free volume and lowering the pressure.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point: finite molecular volume actually INCREASES pressure above ideal (less free space for molecules). The reason pressure is BELOW ideal is intermolecular attractions, not molecular volume.

required_elements: CO$_2$ molecules experience attractive intermolecular forces (LDF and dipole-induced dipole or dipole-dipole).
These attractions reduce the effective momentum of molecules as they approach the wall → they hit the wall with less force → actual pressure < ideal pressure.
alternatives.correct_example: The attractive intermolecular forces between CO$_2$ molecules pull molecules back slightly before they hit the wall, reducing the force of impact. The ideal gas law assumes no IMFs, but real CO$_2$ molecules attract each other. This attraction reduces the effective pressure compared to the ideal prediction.

wrong_examples: Saying molecular volume reduces pressure — finite molecular volume actually INCREASES pressure above ideal by reducing available free space. The decrease in pressure is due to attractions, not volume.
Saying ideal gas assumes no collisions — ideal gas assumes elastic collisions, not no collisions.
common_traps: Real vs. ideal deviations: IMF attractions → P$_{real}$ < P$_{ideal}$ (molecules pulled back from walls). Finite molecular volume → P$_{real}$ > P$_{ideal}$ (less free space). CO$_2$ at moderate pressure: attraction effect dominates → P < ideal.
